mappings.rationaleAI: The 1937-1945 Sino-Japanese War is central to both the upsurge of strife in Asia during the 1930s (A.3.a) and the Pacific theatre of World War II, covering Japanese aggression from the seizure of Manchuria through Pearl Harbor and Allied counteroffensive leading to Japanese defeat (B.2.a, B.2.b).

U.S. military, economic, and diplomatic dominance after 1945, Cold War interventionism, decolonization, and the global spread of American influence map directly to the continuing Cold War (C.2.f), postwar economic dominance (C.3.a.i), and decolonization (C.3.a.ii).

Lenin's establishment of the one-party Soviet state, War Communism, and the New Economic Policy are explicitly referenced in A.4.c, while his revolutionary model informed the failed socialist movements across postwar Europe (A.1.a).

The 1936-1939 Spanish Civil War as an international proxy struggle involving Nazi Germany, Fascist Italy, and the Soviet Union is the core subject of A.3.b (Popular Front and the Spanish Civil War), foreshadowing the broader European confrontation between fascism, communism, and democracy.

Analysis of how nuclear deterrence, Cold War confrontation, and brutal conflicts in Africa and Eastern Europe have transformed warfare connects to the balance of terror (C.2.f.viii), areas of conflict including sub-Saharan Africa (C.3.c.iii), international terrorism (C.3.c.iv), and the burgeoning of military technology (B.6).

The British Empire's dissolution by 1960, driven by nationalist movements and two world wars, directly addresses increased demands for self-determination among subject peoples (A.3.c) and the sweeping process of decolonization after 1957 (C.3.a.ii).

Hindenburg's presidency of the Weimar Republic and his 1933 appointment of Hitler as Chancellor directly address the failure of democratic governments in central Europe (A.1.a) and the establishment of Nazi Germany (A.4.c).

The failed Spartacist uprising of January 1919 and Luxemburg's murder by Freikorps paramilitaries exemplify the failure of attempts to establish socialist governments in postwar Europe that defines A.1.a, marking the violent suppression of revolutionary movements in Weimar Germany.

mappings.episodeTitle: The Economic Consequences of the Peace
mappings.rationaleAI: Keynes's critique of the Versailles Treaty's punitive reparations addresses post-Paris Peace Conference diplomacy (A.1.b), its influence on British appeasement policy connects to the failure of diplomatic attempts to avert war (A.3.b), and the predicted economic catastrophe relates to the Great Depression's political impact (A.4.b).

The gold standard's rigidity constrained monetary policy during the interwar period (A.4.a) and its role in deepening the Great Depression through enforced deflation is widely cited as a central cause of the 1930s economic collapse (A.4.b).

The 1919 protests against the Treaty of Versailles's transfer of Shandong to Japan connect to post-Paris Peace Conference diplomacy (A.1.b), and the movement's role in founding the Chinese Communist Party in 1921 is a direct precursor to civil conflict in China (A.3.a).

The development of atomic weapons during WWII represents the most consequential advance in military technology (B.6), and the bombs dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki inaugurated the nuclear era that defined postwar international relations and the balance of terror (C.2.a.i).

The episode covers 20th-century mass atrocities across two world wars, the Holocaust, Stalinist Russia, Vietnam, Korea, and Rwanda -- all topics of international conflict, diplomacy, and war since 1920 (A, B, C).
The Spanish Civil War and China's wars with Japan in the 1930s, which Auden observed and wrote about, are international conflicts of the interwar period (A, B).

The 50th anniversary of the UN Declaration of Human Rights situates this episode within post-WWII international relations frameworks, including debates about universal values vs cultural particularism in a postcolonial context (C).
